reasoning:
The FRED tool statistical models assign a ~0.70-0.73 probability for the 30-year mortgage rate exceeding 6.65 by early April, grounded in linear trend projections since September 2024 and prior-year seasonal shifts. The 10-year Treasury yield also experienced slight upward pressure leading into late March (climbing to 4.38% on March 27), suggesting upcoming survey periods will carry short-term upward momentum above the 6.65 threshold. 

However, looking further into 2025 and early 2026, many market forecasts expect mortgage rates to ease closer to the low-6% or high-5% range, mostly driven by anticipated, steady Federal Reserve rate cuts. Counterbalancing this expectation are ongoing term-premium concerns regarding the national deficit and sticky inflation, which may keep long-end rates elevated.

Given the explicit prompt instruction that uncertainty should increase over time and probabilities for distant dates should converge closer to 0.5, I start with a relatively high probability (0.65) for the near-term April dates influenced by recent yield bumps and the FRED statistical tool. I then strictly step the probabilities down towards 0.50 for the longer-term resolution dates, reflecting the balance between potential long-rate elevation and expected future Fed easing.
